item.title: STAFF POST OPEN - CROSSLINK PROPOSAL
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

Show Proposal Reasoning
Hide Reasoning

Crosslinking article to other Assyrian Mythological articles.

Editing this:

SCP-2527-1 is a 2m high stone statue of a Lamassu.1

To this:

SCP-2527-1 is a 2m high stone statue of a Lamassu.2

Footnotes
1. Assyrian mythological deity with the head of a bearded human, the body of a lion, and wings
2. Assyrian mythological deity with the head of a bearded human, the body of a lion, and wings

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it might be made less confusing if you describe the commodore 64 before the statue. The reference to impregnation seems unnecessary and a bit off-putting, and I think the list of things the statue can do should include those seen in the test. Speaking of the test, it might be a good idea to also describe what was happening while the game was played. This is a really cool concept, though.

item.title: Ehh...
item.content:encoded: I get why it's popular, but the way the text is written (I've seen it called "totleighspeak", which I guess is the best term for it) just doesn't do it for me. It's too silly to be scary, but it's also not silly enough to be entertaining to me in any way. I'm downvoting, but I think I could be swayed to novoting…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is positively hilarious, but I feel this should be J material instead of main series. Would upvote if it's moved to such category.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Where's the Totleighsoft logo on this puppy? It seems like their kind of product, although the stone peripheral device is a bit unusual for them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I pray to MASSY that this comes up in the current crosslinking project.
MASSY are the levelup! !!!! Continue y/n? > y

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I initially upvoted, but then my pedantry kicked in. I wanna know more about this, and I feel the Foundation would put more information about this in the document. No vote, but I like it, if that makes any sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh dear, that's embarrassing. Sorry, I'll try to read more carefully next time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's already a footnote after "lamassu" that states it's an Assyrian diety.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quick googling of "massy" and "assyrian" led me to an article about Lamassu, the Assyrian protective deity. I recommend you mention this somewhere in the article, because the idea between the video game and the statue is too disconnected right now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I personally feel that this is probably a bootleg of a game designed for young gods like SCP-2662 (probably younger ones). I liked it BTW.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The WTF is strong with this one.
Done well you have.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The writing in the log is really well done and basically my entire reason for upvoting. Very creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm actually surprised there's not more to this. I mean, it feels really short, yet with lots of potential. Not enough to downvote, though I have this comment:

Incinerating organic material,

How? The next entry describes emitting a shockwave, so wouldn't this necessitate fire? By which I mean, it'd be more clinical to say that it can emit fire, and perhaps a length and/or temperature of the flame.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is a difference between having some mystery in the article and not explaining anything. This one falls in the latter, and I'm not a fan of "it's mysterious because you don't know anything" kinda article because it feels lazy. Is the idea interesting and has potential? Sure. But then again, a lot of articles do, and just an interesting idea isn't enough. The way it is right now, this is the skeleton of an article that still needs some meat added to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I'm in love with this, I would recommend some recovery log or other backstory to explain how the foundation came to obtain the scp, and how they figured out its anomalous properties. I think something like this would change some minds who were initially downvoting.

+1 still

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find this disturbingly cute, I used to play a bunch of IF games, I kinda would like to see more experiments with different commands.

Also where was this found?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
FIRE INTENTS!
Burning is multiple.
D-3515 screams profuse.

That, specifically, made me upvote.

+1 is multiple

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's magic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Decibelle said something to this effect, and I'm starting to ponder on this. It'd be hard to determine where the line is between "not enough information" and "too much information."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a bit torn on this one. On one hand, I absolutely love the idea of a god/deity being controlled by a video game, and something about the horribly incorrect grammar within the game's text is rather unsettling. It brings up some very good questions; who were the people who made this game? Why is it so badly bootlegged? Is there an original, higher-quality version out there somewhere? I really like skips that cane get me to ask questions like that. However, I feel the article is a little too short for its own good. I personally would've preferred if there were a clinical, side-by-side description of what was happening to the D-Class as the commands are being inputted, since I feel it would help hammer home the uncanniness of the game's cheery text as well as provide some squick factor. But some people might prefer the "show don't tell" type thing that the game's text provides, so I dunno. Staying neutral on this one for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will add it to the list…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Then it's your turn to write!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not what I was thinking actually…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not a fan of this article. I don't see the connection between a statue and a text adventure game, and the log really doesn't do anything for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kinda reminds me of Temmie from Undertale

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I legit laughed out loud.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't you need a (sic) or two in there, to indicate that the misspellings are how the program actually reads?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you insist, the basic idea is now in my sandbox, under the "daveyoufool" tab.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: oh, now I'm curious!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It does feel like poorly-translated bootleg software, which makes me wonder if there's an original program in a different language somewhere — and what other functions that program might have. I'd expect the bootleg version to have missing or badly-translated commands, for instance, so that the lammasu might be capable of other, stranger things if we only knew what the commands were.

Also, this:

Punish D-3515 is possible.

The user enters "burn d-3515" in response, but what would happen if they entered "punish d-3515"? Could be the same, could be much different.

And that's why I like this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if you are thinking what I thought when I read it…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
upvoted but it still shows up as [ACCESS DENIED] on the main page
good concept and title +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well…

I've already found a cross test I want to write up with this, so +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This just doesn't interest me at all.

'It looks like a powerful and mysterious anomaly that should be taken seriously… but HAH! It's all a cosmic joke!' is a device that I feel has been beaten into the ground by this point, and this just has nothing else to it beyond the reveal that it's all a joke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Let's see… "thing what kills you," "thing what lets you fight good," "indestructible thing"… This proves that even the most cliched concepts can still dusted off and made fresh, new, interesting, and mysterious.

Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After all, it wouldn't be fair if your character could die between game sessions, now would it?

The main character can always respawn after death. But yeah, main characters are always invincible badasses in-context (unless you go multi-player).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Could also be indestructible because nobody tried hurting it while the game is running.

After all, it wouldn't be fair if your character could die between game sessions, now would it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It would be more interesting if previous users save-scum their way to prevent SCP-2527's godly offspring from being born (and overthrow it).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: because of savescumming I assume.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a few things that bothered me, like making it indestructible and the whole "undetermined form of energy" thing (a shockwave is just an atmospheric pressure wave, you could have said something like "shockwave of indeterminate origin"), but I really liked how it straddles the line between creepy and funny.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If anything, this aesthetically reminds me of a Pokemon game… but with gods.

That said, why must it be another indestructible thing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll admit that while this hinges mostly on the log at the end, I thought it amusing and reminiscent enough of my experiences with bootlegged games and text adventures that it won me over. The article being as short as it is helps with it too. While I think you could expand on the possible connections between SCP-2527-1 and -2 (and I do encourage you to do so), being too in-depth or explanatory about it would probably weaken the article, as taking away a good amount of mystery would hurt the quality.

I do think it's a worthwhile angle to explore if you continue to update the article, and see how to strengthen it, as it can come off as being disparate and unrelated (and I assume you have an idea of why these two are connected). As a whole though, it amuses me and the log absolutely made it for me.
